entries.summary: The Boost Software License's distinguishing clause is its carve-out for binary distribution: the copyright notice and license statement 'must be included in all copies of the Software, in whole or in part, and all derivative works of the Software, unless such copies or derivative works are solely in the form of machine-executable object code generated by a source language processor.' Boost's own rationale for the license explicitly frames this as the reason to pick BSL over MIT, BSD, or Apache, all of which contain a notice-preservation requirement with no binary exemption. Boost notes that the BSL waiver is precisely what lets the license be used in vendor-shipped binaries (their cited example: Microsoft's STL implementation) where customers cannot tolerate a binary attribution obligation. The waiver is strictly for binary-only distribution: source or mixed source-plus-binary distributions still carry the notice requirement.

sources.source: Boost user guide — Boost Software License
sources.excerpt: The copyright notices in the Software and this entire statement, including the above license grant, this restriction and the following disclaimer, must be included in all copies of the Software, in whole or in part, and all derivative works of the Software, unless such copies or derivative works are solely in the form of machine-executable object code generated by a source language processor. … All popular licenses — MIT, Apache, BSD — contain language similar to the following … 'The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software.' … And, in fact, so does the Boost license, except it continues with: 'unless such copies or derivative works are solely in the form of machine-executable object code generated by a source language processor.' The others contain no such exemption.

sources.source: Boost user guide — Boost Software License
